disclaimer: Impact is qualitative (likely / possible). This report makes no build-time predictions; measure before/after in your own environment to confirm any change.

findings.title: Default features enabled on `featured`
findings.diagnosis: `app` depends on `featured` with default features left on; its `default` feature turns on: extra. If those features are unused, they add compile work for no benefit.

steps: Confirm the default features are actually unused: `cargo tree -e features -i featured` shows what enables each feature.
In `app`'s Cargo.toml set `default-features = false` for `featured`, then re-add only the features you use: `featured = { version = "…", default-features = false, features = ["…"] }`.
Build and test to confirm nothing you rely on was behind a default feature.

next_tool.name: cargo tree -e features -i
next_tool.purpose: trace which crate or feature enables each default feature

findings.title: Proc-macro crates in the build graph
findings.diagnosis: 1 proc-macro crate(s) are compiled and executed at build time. Proc-macros run during every compile of their dependents and can dominate front-end time.

steps: Check whether each proc-macro is essential; some derives have lighter alternatives, or can be replaced by a hand-written impl in hot crates.
Measure front-end cost with `cargo llvm-lines` before removing anything — inspect, do not guess.
Keep proc-macro-heavy dependencies in a stable lower layer so they are not recompiled when your own code changes.

next_tool.name: cargo-llvm-lines
next_tool.purpose: reveal which generic and macro-generated code expands the most

references.label: The Rust Performance Book — Compile times

findings.title: Build scripts (build.rs) in the build graph
findings.diagnosis: 1 crate(s) run a build script. build.rs runs at build time and is INVISIBLE to RUSTC_WRAPPER timing, so its cost never appears in measured rustc numbers.

steps: Confirm each build script is required; some exist only for optional features you may not use.
Prefer crates that gate native or codegen build scripts behind features, and disable those features when unused.
For your own build scripts, cache expensive work and emit precise `cargo:rerun-if-changed` lines so they do not re-run needlessly.
